title: Conjecture 1 (Berger et al.)
statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that every $t$-local tournament $T$ satisfies $\vec{\chi}(T) \leq f(t)$.
context_text: A tournament is $t$-local if for every vertex $v$ the subtournament induced by the out-neighbors of $v$ has chromatic number at most $t$. The conjecture was raised in Berger et al. [3] as Conjecture 2.6 and settled for $t = 2$ in [7]; the present paper proves it for all $t$.
attributed_to: Berger, Choromanski, Chudnovsky, Fox, Loebl, Scott, Seymour, and Thomassé
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture for all $t$: every $t$-local tournament has chromatic number at most $f(t) = \max\{(t+1)K,\,(t+1)\ell\}$ for explicit constants $K$ and $\ell$ depending only on $t$, derived via a new result on dominating number and bounded-size subtournaments.
notes: PDF source — directed chromatic number notation ~χ interpreted as $\vec{\chi}$.
paper_title: Coloring tournaments: from local to global

title: Problem 3
statement_text: For every integer $i \geq 1$, there exists $f(i)$ such that every tournament $T$ with dominating number at least $f(i)$ contains an isomorphic copy of $S_i$.
context_text: The tournaments $S_i$ are defined recursively: $S_1$ is a single vertex, and for $i > 1$, $S_i$ is the tournament on $2^i - 1$ vertices obtained by blowing up two vertices of an oriented triangle into two copies of $S_{i-1}$; one can check $\vec{\chi}(S_i) \geq i$. The problem is trivial for $i \leq 2$, verified for $i = 3$ in [7], and open for all $i \geq 4$.
notes: PDF source — math notation may be partially garbled; recursive definition of $S_i$ reconstructed from surrounding text.
paper_title: Coloring tournaments: from local to global

title: Problem 4
statement_text: For every integer $k \geq 1$, there exist integers $K$ and $\ell$ such that every tournament $T$ with dominating number at least $K$ contains a subtournament with $\ell$ vertices and dominating number at least $k$.
context_text: Theorem 2 shows that sufficiently large dominating number forces a bounded-size subtournament of large chromatic number. The analogous statement with dominating number in place of chromatic number in the conclusion remains open; the note observes that replacing dominating-number hypothesis by large chromatic number alone fails for any $k \geq 3$.
paper_title: Coloring tournaments: from local to global

title: Conjecture 7 (Scott and Seymour)
statement_text: For every $k$, there exists $K$ such that if $T$ and $G$ are respectively a tournament and a graph on the same set of vertices with $G$ of chromatic number at least $K$, then there is a vertex $v$ such that $G$ induces on $N^+_T(v)$ a subgraph of $G$ of chromatic number at least $k$.
context_text: Motivated by the paper's main result on out-neighborhoods in tournaments, the authors observe that a suitable notion of 'large' hypergraph would imply a statement about high-chromatic graphs and tournament out-neighborhoods; they note that a very similar conjecture was independently proposed by Alex Scott and Paul Seymour (personal communication [14]).
paper_contribution: Mentioned as a related open conjecture inspired by the paper's main results; the paper provides motivation via the notion of 'large' hypergraphs but offers no proof or partial progress.
notes: Attribution via personal communication [14]; no publication year available.
paper_title: Coloring tournaments: from local to global
